Symmetric structure — compare char at L with char at R, move inward. If they always match, it's a palindrome. Natural fi
Width decreases as pointers converge, so you must move the shorter wall inward — it's the only pointer that could yield
Fix one element, then two-pointer the sorted remainder. Sorting enables skipping duplicates and the L/R convergence redu
Negative numbers have large squares on the left, positives on the right. Converge from both ends, picking the larger squ
Slow pointer tracks where the next non-zero should go. Fast scans all elements. Non-zeros get written at slow; remaining

Check if any value appears twice within a window of size k. Sliding window + set.
Fixed window of size k, check if average meets threshold. Direct sliding window sum check.
Expand right to satisfy the constraint (contain all target chars). Once satisfied, shrink left to minimize length. Frequ
Expand right freely. When distinct count exceeds K, shrink left until it drops back to K. A frequency map tracks counts;

Longest subarray with at most 2 distinct values. Variable window with a frequency map — shrink left when distinct count

Find index where left sum equals right sum. Total prefix sum lets you check leftSum == totalSum - leftSum - nums[i] at e

At each position, decide: extend the current subarray or start fresh. If the running sum goes negative, it can only hurt
Sorted = monotonic. Compare middle element to target: if smaller, discard left half; if larger, discard right half. Halv

When the answer space is monotonic ("is X feasible?" flips from false→true), binary search over the answer itself. Test
Not fully sorted, but locally monotonic. If mid < mid+1, a peak exists to the right; otherwise to the left. Binary searc
Classic "search on answer" — binary search over eating speed k. For each k, check if Koko finishes in time (feasibility
Binary search on capacity [max(w)..sum(w)]. For each candidate, greedily pack weights into days. Monotonic: higher capac
Find index where target would be inserted in sorted array. Classic binary search returning lo when not found.
Only 3 distinct values — don't need a full sort. Three pointers carve out regions for 0s, 1s, and 2s in a single pass. O
Two categories (even/odd) = simplified Dutch flag with just 2 groups. One pointer tracks the boundary, other scans — swa

First pass: build frequency map. Second pass: scan left-to-right, return first char with count=1. Two O(n) passes. The m
Boyer-Moore Voting: maintain a candidate and count. Same element → count++, different → count--. When count hits 0, swit
All anagrams share the same sorted-character key (or frequency signature). Use that as a HashMap key to bucket strings.

For each element, you need the first larger element to its right. A decreasing stack keeps candidates. When a new elemen

For each bar, you need the nearest shorter bar on both sides. An increasing stack tracks bars. When a shorter bar arrive
"How many consecutive days was price ≤ today?" = distance to previous greater element. Decreasing stack of indices; when
Greedily remove digits that break non-decreasing order (left to right). Monotonic increasing stack: if incoming digit <
Find next smaller element for each position. Monotonic increasing stack — mirror of next greater element.
Median = element at index n/2 in sorted order. Quick Select finds this in O(n) average without sorting the entire array.
Array has values in range [0,n] or [1,n] — each value has a "home" index. Place each number at its home, then scan for t
Ignore negatives and values > n. Place valid positives at index (val-1). After placement, the first index where nums[i]
Triple reversal trick: reverse all, reverse first k, reverse rest. Each reversal is O(n) in-place. Avoids the naive O(n·
Reverse the entire string, then reverse each word individually. The double reversal cancels out within each word but fli
Transpose (swap rows↔cols) then reverse each row = 90° clockwise. Both operations are in-place O(n²). Decomposing rotati
Find rightmost ascending pair (i, i+1), swap nums[i] with the smallest larger element to its right, then reverse the suf
Sorting by start time groups overlapping intervals adjacently. Then a single greedy pass merges: if current.start ≤ last
Already sorted list: add all intervals before the new one, merge all that overlap with it, then add all after. Three pha
Merge all busy intervals, then the gaps between merged intervals are the free slots. Same merge pattern, just extract th
Naive search is O(n·m) — backtracking on mismatch. KMP uses the failure function to skip ahead: partial matches tell you
Map stores char→count in O(n). Query any character's count in O(1). For lowercase only, IntArray(26) is faster; for Unic
Two strings are anagrams iff they have identical frequency maps. Build both maps in O(n), compare in O(k) where k = dist
Build frequency map in O(n), then sort entries by value and take top K. Or use a min-heap of size K for O(n log K). Buck
Build frequency map, then sort entries by count descending. Reconstruct string by repeating each char by its count. Buck
Three HashSets per row/col/box track seen digits. For each cell, check if digit already in row set, col set, or box set.
Instead of O(n²) brute force checking all pairs, store each number's index in a HashMap. For each new number, check if i
Use frequency map instead of index map. For each number, add freq[target - num] to count, then increment freq[num]. Hand
Sort the array O(n log n), then for each element, use two-sum (via two pointers or HashMap) on the remainder. Skip dupli
Four arrays A,B,C,D — count tuples where a+b+c+d=0. Store all a+b sums in a HashMap, then for each c+d pair check if -(c
Prefix sum + HashMap: store prefix_sum → count. At each index, check if (currentSum - k) exists in map → those are subar
Fixed-size sliding window (size = pattern length). Compare window's frequency map to pattern's frequency map at each pos
Fixed-size window (size = pattern length) with frequency map comparison. Slide window, maintain char counts. When window
Recursive problems where the same inputs are computed repeatedly (Fibonacci, grid paths, knapsack). HashMap caches resul
Any pure function (same input → same output) with costly computation benefits from caching. Store input→result in a Hash
DFS on graphs or trees with shared subtrees. Memoize (node, state) → result to avoid re-exploring. Use a data class key
Classic DP: how many ways to reach step n / minimum coins for amount. Overlapping subproblems → HashMap cache turns O(2ⁿ
Can string s be segmented into dictionary words? Memoize index → boolean. At each position, try all dictionary words as
Evict the least frequently used (ties broken by least recent). HashMap + frequency buckets (each a LinkedHashSet). More
Insert/delete/getMax/getMin all in O(1). HashMap for key→node lookup, doubly linked list of frequency buckets. Each buck
Maintain a sliding window HashSet of size K. As you iterate, add the current element. If add() returns false → duplicate
Use two sets: "seen" and "duplicates". For each element, if seen.add() returns false, add to duplicates set. O(n) time,
LinkedHashMap preserves insertion order. Build frequency map in one pass, then iterate entries — first with count 1 is t
Every element appears twice except one. XOR approach is O(1) space, but set approach works too: add if unseen, remove if
Find middle with fast/slow, reverse second half, compare with first half. Uses reversal as a sub-routine for O(1) space
If there's a cycle, fast and slow must meet (gap shrinks by 1 each step). If no cycle, fast reaches null first. O(n) tim
Sum of squared digits eventually loops or reaches 1. Use fast/slow on the digit-sum sequence instead of a HashSet — O(1)
Dummy head + two pointers. Compare heads, attach smaller, advance that pointer. When one is exhausted, attach the remain
Merge sort is ideal for linked lists — find middle (fast/slow), split, recursively sort, merge. O(n log n) time, O(log n
Advance fast n+1 steps ahead of slow (starting from dummy). When fast reaches null, slow is just before the target. One
Virtual concatenation (A+B and B+A) aligns both pointers at the intersection. When one reaches null, redirect to the oth
Find middle (fast/slow), reverse second half, compare with first half. O(n) time, O(1) space. The classic three-pattern
Same middle + reverse + compare approach works whenever you need to verify the first half mirrors the second half. Stack
Three-step combo: find middle (fast/slow), reverse second half, interleave both halves. O(n) time, O(1) space. The inter
Use stacks to process digits from least significant, or reverse both lists first, add, then reverse the result. Stack ap
Two dummy heads collect nodes into less-than and greater-than-or-equal chains. Connect them at the end. Preserves relati
Find length and tail, normalize k (k % length), make circular, walk to new tail at (length - k - 1), break the circle. O
First check if k nodes remain (return as-is if not). Reverse k nodes with the standard three-pointer technique. Recursiv
HashMap maps old→new nodes. Pass 1: create copies. Pass 2: wire next and random via the map. O(n) time, O(n) space. Inte
Same HashMap (old→clone) pattern generalizes to any structure with arbitrary pointers. Two passes: create all nodes, the
HashMap for O(1) key lookup, DLL for O(1) access-order tracking. On get: move to front. On put: add to front, evict tail
Extend LRU with frequency buckets. Each frequency has its own DLL. HashMap maps key → node. On access, move node to the
Push openers, pop when closer matches top. If mismatch or stack not empty at end, invalid. Classic stack problem.
Push indices onto stack. Use a sentinel index (-1) to calculate lengths. When matched, length = i - stack.top.
Track unmatched positions in a stack. After processing, stack size = number of removals needed.
Parallel min stack tracks the minimum at each level. Push mirrors main stack with min(current, prevMin). Pop both togeth
Same pattern with max instead of min. Parallel max stack tracks running maximum at each level.
Generalize to any associative operation — sum, GCD, etc. Maintain a parallel stack with the running aggregate.
Convert infix to postfix (Shunting Yard), then evaluate with a stack. Clean separation of parsing and evaluation.
Stack-based evaluation handles arbitrary expression depth naturally. Each operator consumes exactly two operands.
Monotonic stack maintains sorted invariant. When an element violates the invariant, pop and assign — each element pushed
Decreasing stack of indices. When a warmer day appears, pop all cooler days and compute distance.
Decreasing stack tracks previous greater element. Span = distance to that element.
Increasing stack finds left and right boundaries for each bar — the first smaller bar on each side.
Monotonic increasing stack. When a shorter bar is encountered, pop taller bars and compute area. Sentinel at end forces
Decreasing stack. When a taller bar appears, pop and calculate water trapped between current bar and the bar below the p
Two-pointer approach is more common, but stack-based thinking helps understand the bounding walls concept.
Classic monotonic decreasing stack. Push indices of unresolved days. When a warmer day appears, pop and compute distance
Generalization: any "how far until condition X" maps to a monotonic stack with distance calculation.
Split by "/", push directory names, ".." pops (go up), "." and "" are no-ops. Join stack with "/" for canonical path.
Stack naturally models directory hierarchy — push to descend, pop to ascend.
Two stacks (counts + strings) handle nesting. '[' pushes current state, ']' pops and repeats. Natural recursive structur
Each '[' starts a new scope. Stack depth = nesting level. Inner patterns resolve first (LIFO matches nesting).
'(' pushes current result + sign onto stack, ')' pops and combines. Parentheses create scopes that the stack manages nat
* and / process immediately (pop, compute, push). + and - defer by pushing. Final sum of stack gives result. Higher prec
Stack simulates collisions: positive = right, negative = left. Collision only when top is positive and current is negati
Any problem where elements moving in opposite directions cancel each other. Stack tracks surviving elements.
Monotonic increasing stack. Greedily remove digits that are greater than the next digit — this produces the smallest pos
Generalization: build the lexicographically smallest/largest sequence by greedily maintaining monotonic order with a rem
Explicit stack replaces the call stack. Avoids StackOverflowError on deep graphs/trees. Same time complexity as recursiv
JVM default stack depth ~5000-10000 frames. For trees with depth > 5000, iterative DFS is required.
Heap memory is much larger than stack memory. Explicit stack uses heap, supporting millions of elements.
Monotonic decreasing stack. Span = number of consecutive days with price ≤ today's price. Pop all smaller/equal, span =
The element remaining on top of the stack after popping is the previous greater element. Span = distance to it.
BFS explores by distance. All neighbors at distance d are visited before d+1, so the first time you reach a node is the
Start BFS from each unvisited land cell to flood-fill the entire island. Each BFS = one connected component.
Each BFS level = one move. First arrival at target = fewest moves. Works for grid puzzles, state-space search.
BFS radiates outward uniformly. The first target found is guaranteed to be the closest one.
BFS visits every node exactly once. Use a HashMap to map original→clone, wiring neighbors as you go.
BFS with 2-coloring. Alternate colors at each level. If a neighbor has the same color → not bipartite.
Process all nodes at depth d before depth d+1 using queue.size snapshot per level.
BFS finds the first leaf node — that's the shallowest leaf = minimum depth. Faster than DFS which must explore all paths
All rotten oranges start spreading simultaneously. Enqueue all rotten cells, BFS radiates in parallel — each level = 1 m
Enqueue all gates, BFS fills each empty room with distance to nearest gate. Multi-source = all gates radiate simultaneou
Virtual super-source connected to all start points with 0-weight edges. One BFS covers all.
Enqueue all 0-cells, BFS outward. Each 1-cell gets filled with its distance to the nearest 0 on first visit.
Reverse multi-source BFS from each ocean border inward. Cells reachable from both oceans are the answer.
Implicit graph: words are nodes, edges connect words differing by 1 letter. BFS finds shortest transformation.
State = lock combination (4-digit string). Each turn = 1 edge. BFS from "0000" to target avoiding deadends.
Same as word ladder but with gene strings (ACGT). BFS through valid mutations in the gene bank.
State = board configuration. Each move = swap blank with neighbor. BFS through state space for shortest solution.
Monotonic decreasing deque: front = window max. Remove expired from front, remove smaller from back. O(n) total.
Same pattern but monotonic increasing deque. Front = window min.
Identical to sliding window maximum. Output result when window reaches size k.
Monotonic deque on prefix sums. Remove from front when valid, from back when not useful. Handles negative numbers.
DP + monotonic deque: dp[i] = nums[i] + max(dp[j]) for j in window. Deque tracks max dp value in sliding window.
Classic design question. Tests amortized analysis. Two-stack lazy transfer achieves O(1) amortized per operation.
Queue holds the last k values. On each new value: enqueue, dequeue oldest if over k. Running sum gives O(1) average.
Queue of timestamps. Dequeue expired hits (older than 5 minutes). Queue size = hit count in window.
Queue of request timestamps. If queue size ≥ limit within time window → reject. Dequeue expired entries.
Queue stores call timestamps. On each call, dequeue entries older than 3000ms. Queue size = count.
Grid cells have direction arrows. Following costs 0, changing costs 1. 0/1 weights → 01-BFS instead of Dijkstra.
When movement costs are 0 (same terrain) or 1 (obstacle bypass), 01-BFS is O(V+E) vs Dijkstra's O((V+E) log V).
Deque replaces priority queue: 0-weight → front (same distance), 1-weight → back (one more). Linear time.
Greedy: always run the most frequent available task. Max-heap picks it, cooldown queue holds it until ready. Minimizes i
Place most frequent char, wait k positions before reusing. Max-heap + cooldown queue enforces the gap.
Min-heap of (a[i]+b[j], i, j). Poll and expand neighbors. Lazily explores pairs in sorted order.
Modified Dijkstra/Bellman-Ford with stop limit. Track (cost, node, stops) in priority queue.
Grid cells have elevation. Min-heap BFS: always expand the cell with lowest elevation. First to reach (n-1,n-1) = answer
Shortest root-to-leaf path. Must handle single-child nodes (not leaves). BFS is more efficient — stops at first leaf.
Diameter = farthest pair. One DFS computes all heights and tracks max left+right sum.
Return height or -1 (unbalanced). One DFS, O(n). Avoids the naive O(n²) of computing height separately at each node.
Compare left subtree with mirror of right. Recursive: left.left↔right.right AND left.right↔right.left.
BFS level-order: once a non-full node is seen, all subsequent nodes must be leaves.
Two trees are flip-equivalent if one can become the other by swapping children at some nodes. Recursive check with both
Same as path sum but collect ALL paths. Backtrack: add node to path, recurse, remove after.
Prefix sum + HashMap on tree paths. If currentSum - target exists in map → valid subpath. Backtrack map entry after subt
Like intersection of two linked lists: walk both pointers up, they meet at LCA. O(h) time, O(1) space.
Preorder[0] = root. Find root in inorder → everything left = left subtree, right = right subtree. Recurse. O(n) with Has
BFS: last node in each level is the rightmost visible. Or DFS right-first: first node at each new depth is the answer.
BFS level-by-level, reverse every other level. Queue.size snapshot separates levels cleanly.
BFS level snapshot: track max per level. Single pass, no sorting needed.
Convert tree to right-pointer-only chain in preorder. Morris-style: find rightmost of left subtree, link to right subtre
Inorder traversal of BST = sorted order. Stop at kth element. O(h + k) time.
Controlled inorder using a stack: pushAllLeft, then pop+process+pushAllLeft(right). O(h) space, O(1) amortized next().
Binary search down the tree tracking closest so far. O(h) — no need for full traversal.
DP on tree: each node returns (rob, skip). Rob = val + skip(left) + skip(right). Skip = max of children's rob/skip.
Recursively compare: both null → true. One null → false. Values equal AND both subtrees same → true. O(min(n,m)).
Overlapping nodes: sum values. Non-overlapping: take whichever exists. Natural recursive structure.
Collect leaf sequences via DFS on both trees, compare the sequences. Uses same isSameTree building block.
Assign column to each node (root=0, left=col-1, right=col+1). BFS + TreeMap groups by column. Sort by row within column.
Left boundary (top-down) + leaves (DFS) + right boundary (bottom-up). Combines vertical/level thinking.
startsWith(prefix) in O(L). Then DFS from prefix node to enumerate all completions. Foundation of search bars.
Build trie from word list, DFS the board following trie edges. Prune branches not in trie — dramatically faster than che
O(log n) update + O(log n) prefix sum query. Simpler and faster constant than segment tree for sum-only problems.
Process right-to-left, BIT indexed by value. Query prefix sum up to current value = count of smaller elements to the rig
BFS naturally finds shortest paths because it explores layer by layer. Each level = one more edge.
Queue processes one complete layer before the next. Perfect for tree level-order, minimum moves.
BFS finds the closest target first. Stop as soon as target is dequeued.
Back edges in DFS tree indicate cycles. Use three-color for directed graphs.
DFS reverse postorder gives topological ordering of a DAG.
Each DFS from an unvisited vertex discovers one component.
DFS explores full paths. Good for "can we reach X from Y?"
DFS naturally explores, undoes, and tries alternatives.
Each BFS/DFS from an unvisited vertex = one component. O(V+E).
DFS parent check or Union-Find. Visited non-parent neighbor = cycle.
Three-color DFS (back edge to GRAY node) or Kahn's topological sort (processed < n means cycle).
Greedy with min-heap. Process each vertex once. Standard for weighted graphs.
Grow MST from a starting node using a priority queue. Best for dense graphs.
Each DFS/BFS from unvisited '1' discovers one island. Mark visited to avoid re-counting.
Same DFS, but return the count of cells visited. Track maximum.
dp[n] = dp[n-1] + dp[n-2]. The canonical DP example. Memo turns O(2^n) → O(n). Rolling vars → O(1) space.
Identical to fibonacci: ways to reach step n = ways to reach n-1 + ways to reach n-2. dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2].
dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]). Same recurrence but with a cost layer on top.
Generalize: dp[i] = sum of previous k values. Inner loop over step sizes.
Can't pick adjacent elements. dp[i] = max(skip, take). Classic "include/exclude with adjacency constraint."
DP on tree: return (rob, skip) pair per node. Rob = val + skip(children). Skip = max(rob, skip) per child.
Same take/skip pattern with constraint. dp tracks best ending with include vs exclude.
dp[amount] = min over coins of dp[amount - coin] + 1. Unbounded knapsack variant — each coin reusable.
dp[day] = min cost to cover travel through day. Try 1-day, 7-day, 30-day passes at each travel day.
dp[i] = LIS ending at i. O(n²) DP or O(n log n) with patience sorting (tails + binary search).
Track both length and count at each position. When dp[j]+1 == dp[i], add count[j] to count[i].
Sort by second element, then LIS on first elements. Or greedy like activity selection.
Same as LIS but use ≤ instead of <. Binary search uses upper_bound instead of lower_bound.
Kadane's: dp[i] = max(nums[i], dp[i-1]+nums[i]). Extend current subarray or start fresh. O(n), O(1) space.
Like climbing stairs but only valid 1-2 digit codes (1-26). dp[i] += dp[i-1] if single valid, += dp[i-2] if double valid
dp[i] = can s[0..i-1] be segmented? Check all j: dp[j] && s[j..i] in dict. O(n² × L).
Same DP + backtracking to collect all valid segmentations. Memoize to avoid re-exploring.
Partition string into 4 valid octets (0-255). DP/backtracking with validity constraints at each split.
Greedy: track farthest reachable. If current index > farthest → stuck. O(n), O(1).
BFS-level greedy: each "level" = one jump. Track currentEnd and farthest. Increment jumps when i == currentEnd.
DP with HashSet per stone: track which jump sizes can reach it. For each stone, try k-1, k, k+1.
dp[n] = min(dp[n-k²]+1). Identical to coin change where "coins" are 1,4,9,16,... O(n√n).
DP generating sequence: track pointers for ×2, ×3, ×5. Next ugly = min of three candidates.
dp[n] = max(j × (n-j), j × dp[n-j]) for all j. Break n into parts that maximize product.
Count paths from top-left to bottom-right (right/down only). dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. O(m×n), optimizable to
Same recurrence but dp[i][j] = 0 if grid[i][j] is obstacle.
dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Same grid, minimize instead of count.
Bottom-up: dp[i][j] += min(dp[i+1][j], dp[i+1][j+1]). Triangular grid DP.
dp[i][j] = min(left, top, diagonal) + 1 if cell is '1'. Track largest square of all 1s.
Min insert/delete/replace to transform s1→s2. dp[i][j] = cost for s1[0..i-1]↔s2[0..j-1]. O(m×n).
Same DP structure but cost = ASCII values of deleted chars instead of count.
Count ways s contains t as subsequence. Match → dp[i-1][j-1]+dp[i-1][j], skip → dp[i-1][j].
Each item used at most once. dp[w] iterated RIGHT-TO-LEFT so each item isn't reused within the same pass.
Items reusable. dp[w] iterated LEFT-TO-RIGHT — allows same item to contribute multiple times.
LPS(s) = LCS(s, reverse(s)). Or interval DP: dp[i][j] = LPS of s[i..j]. If s[i]==s[j] → dp[i+1][j-1]+2.
DP: dp[i][j] = true if s[i..j] is palindrome. Or expand-around-center: O(n²) time, O(1) space.
dp[i] = min cuts for s[0..i]. Precompute isPalin[i][j], then dp[i] = min(dp[j-1]+1) for palindromic s[j..i].
'.' = any char, '*' = zero+ of preceding. dp[i][j] = does s[0..i-1] match p[0..j-1]? Handle '*' as zero or more.
'?' = any single char, '*' = any sequence. dp[i][j]: '*' → dp[i-1][j] || dp[i][j-1].
Can s3 be formed by interleaving s1 and s2? dp[i][j] = s3[i+j-1] matches s1[i-1] or s2[j-1] from valid state.
Can a subset sum to target? dp[j] = dp[j] || dp[j-num]. Boolean 0/1 knapsack. O(n × target).
Bitmask DP or backtracking. Track which elements used (mask) and current bucket sum modulo target.
dp[i][j] = min cost to multiply matrices i..j. Try all split points k. O(n³).
Think about which balloon to burst LAST in range [i,j]. dp[i][j] = max coins from range. O(n³).
Merge k consecutive piles. dp[i][j] = min cost. Only mergeable when (j-i) % (k-1) == 0.
dp[i][j] = min cuts for s[i..j]. Interval DP with palindrome precomputation.
Given search frequencies, minimize expected search cost. Try each key as root in range [i..j]. Classic interval DP.
Track min price so far. Profit = price - minSoFar. O(n), O(1). Not really DP — just greedy scan.
Capture every upward move. Greedy: profit += max(0, price[i] - price[i-1]).
dp[t][i] = max profit using t transactions by day i. Track maxDiff for O(k×n) optimization.
dp[mask][i] = min cost visiting cities in mask, ending at i. O(2^n × n²). Only feasible for n ≤ 20.
dp[mask] = sum of elements in mask. Valid if bucket sums work modulo target. O(n × 2^n).
dp[mask][i] = shortest string using words in mask, ending with word i. Precompute overlaps between all pairs.
Bitmask tracks chosen numbers. dp[mask] = can current player force a win? Minimax with memoization.
Bitmask tracks completed tasks. dp[mask] = min time to complete tasks in mask respecting dependencies.
Each root i splits into left (i-1 nodes) × right (n-i nodes). dp[n] = Σ dp[i-1]×dp[n-i]. Catalan numbers.
Same recurrence but build actual trees. For each root, recursively generate all left and right subtrees.
Return (rob, skip) per node. rob = val + skip(children). skip = max(rob, skip) per child. DP on tree structure.
DP on tree: each node tracks best path going left vs going right. Swap direction at each level.
Work BACKWARDS: dp[i][j] = min health needed entering (i,j). Forward doesn't work because you need to survive every step
Two paths simultaneously: dp[r1][c1][r2], c2 derived. Both collectors move in sync, share cherries at same cell.
Two robots from top corners, moving down. dp[row][c1][c2] = max cherries. 3D DP, O(m×n²).
dp[steps][r][c] = number of paths that exit grid. 3D DP with modular arithmetic.
Two-branch recursion: fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2). MUST memoize — naive is O(2^n).
reverse(s) = reverse(s[1..]) + s[0]. Base case: empty or single char. O(n) calls, O(n) stack.
Compare s[left] with s[right], recurse inward. Base case: left ≥ right → true. Converging recursion.
Fix each char at position 0, recursively permute the rest. n! results.
Recurse to tail, then rewire: head.next.next = head, head.next = null. Builds reversed list on unwind. O(n) stack.
Pick smaller head, recurse on remaining. Base case: either list null. Natural recursive structure.
Partition around pivot, recurse on each side. O(n log n) average. In-place but O(n²) worst case.
Max subarray is in left half, right half, or crosses the midpoint. Combine by checking crossing sum.
Not backtracking — use the algorithmic approach (find rightmost ascent, swap, reverse suffix). O(n).
Try digits 1-9 in each empty cell, check row/col/box validity, backtrack on failure. Template 2 (find ANY solution).
Same pattern: try words in each slot, check constraints (intersecting letters), backtrack on conflict.
Match word letter-by-letter on a 2D board. DFS with visited marking + restore. 4-directional. O(m×n×4^L).
Build Trie from word list, DFS the board following Trie edges. Prune branches not in Trie.
Find all paths from start to end. Mark visited, try 4 directions, backtrack. Collect valid paths.
Split digit string into exactly 4 valid octets (0-255, no leading zeros). Fixed-depth backtracking, bounded O(1).
Similar: split digit string into valid 1-2 digit codes (1-26). DP version more efficient, backtracking enumerates all.
